name: all the first gods—lahmu
shortDescription: Lahmu, along with his sister Lahamu, were among the first gods to emerge from the primordial chaos in Babylonian mythology. They represent silt and mud, and are offspring of Apsu and Tiamat.
description: Lahmu, paired with his sister Lahamu, represents one of the first divine beings to originate from the primordial chaos of the Babylonian cosmos. Emerging from the union of Apsu, the freshwater ocean, and Tiamat, the saltwater ocean (often associated with the primordial chaos), Lahmu embodies silt and mud. He is depicted as a large serpent or a bearded man wearing a red sash, reflecting his connection to the earth and the waters. Lahmu and Lahamu represent the foundational elements of the world, the very building blocks upon which subsequent creation would occur. They are crucial figures because their existence marks the first stage of differentiation and order arising from the undifferentiated chaos. They are the parents of Anshar and Kishar, who in turn are the parents of Anu, the sky god, further highlighting their pivotal position in the Babylonian creation myth, the Enuma Elish. Their appearance signifies the initial solidification of the cosmos, a transition from pure watery chaos to a more structured reality. While Lahmu and Lahamu do not play a central active role in later myths, their significance lies in their primordial origin and their essential contribution to the foundation of the Babylonian pantheon and cosmos. They are the necessary precursors to all other gods and the created world, representing the initial separation and solidification of the elements from the primordial waters.

sources.title: Gods, Demons, and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia
sources.author: Jeremy Black and Anthony Green
